items.q: What is the difference between strength and power?
items.correct: Strength is maximum force; power is force times speed of movement.
items.random_wrong: Strength is for the upper body, power for the lower.
items.why: Students conflate strength and power. A strong person can lift heavy slowly; a powerful athlete generates force QUICKLY (sprinter, shot-putter). Power requires both force and velocity.

items.q: Which energy system fuels a marathon?
items.why: Students associate hard exercise with anaerobic systems. Long-duration steady-state work uses AEROBIC metabolism (oxygen + carbs/fats) - efficient but slower to ramp up. Anaerobic systems dominate brief intense efforts.

items.correct: Volume of air inhaled or exhaled in one normal breath at rest.
items.misconception: The maximum air your lungs can hold.
items.random_wrong: Air left in the lungs after maximum exhalation.
items.why: Students confuse tidal volume (resting breath, ~0.5L) with vital capacity (max breath, ~4-5L). Different lung volumes serve different purposes - tidal is the normal small ebb and flow.

items.q: Half of a balanced plate should be:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Vegetables. “Sweets” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's More fibre. “More sugar” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What fraction of the plate is protein?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
